sources.notes: Read the full operation, examples, and limits: t <= 10^4, |s| <= 10^6, total length <= 10^6, modulo 998244353.
Confirmed the interval-endpoint invariant, canonical largest-feasible-endpoint greedy, range-sum DP, and O(n log n) segment-tree optimization.
Independently confirmed deletion of l_p and r_1, the reversed greedy DP, and an O(n log n) Fenwick implementation with a global offset.
Used for the materially different O(n) subsequence-automaton DP compressed by maximal zero-runs.
Cross-checked the O(n) zero-run state compression and difference-array range-add formulation.
Re-derived both transitions; exhaustive BFS over all 1,022 binary strings of length at most 9 matched both reviewed algorithms.

annotation.summary: Count all distinct nonempty binary strings reachable when one operation simultaneously replaces every position before p by the maximum of its adjacent pair and then deletes position p.
annotation.constraints: There are at most 10^4 test cases; each |s| and the sum of |s| are at most 10^6. The 2-second limit rules out explicit reachable-string generation and quadratic endpoint DP.
annotation.core_idea: Represent every current character as max(s[l_i..r_i]). An operation at p deletes l_p from the increasing l-sequence and deletes r_1 from the r-sequence; therefore after q operations, r=(q+1,...,n) while l is any length-(n-q) subsequence of (1,...,n). For a candidate output of length m, reverse it and seek n >= p_1 > ... > p_m >= 1 with bit i equal to max(s[p_i..n-i+1]). Choosing each p_i as large as possible is a canonical feasibility test, so every distinct output has one greedy state trajectory. Let dp[i][j] count length-i reversed outputs whose canonical p_i is j. From a state j, every transition shifts to j-1; when s[j-1..n-i+1] is all zero, choosing output bit 1 instead jumps to pos, the rightmost 1 not past n-i+1, and is available exactly from states j >= pos+2. Hence a layer is one global index shift plus one suffix-sum-to-point update. A Fenwick tree or segment tree with a global coordinate offset maintains this in O(log n) per layer, and summing every layer counts all nonempty lengths.
annotation.complexity: Primary variant: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory per test case. Run-compressed alternative: O(n) time and O(n) memory.
tricks: model each surviving character by an interval maximum
separate one operation into deleting l_p and deleting r_1
canonicalize multiple endpoint witnesses by the largest-feasible greedy choice
treat the DP transition as a global coordinate shift plus one exceptional point update
compress the alternative automaton by maximal zero-runs and difference updates

solution_variants.name: canonical-endpoint-range-sum-dp
solution_variants.summary: Use the interval-endpoint characterization and greedy-largest p_i witness. The endpoint DP applies a global j -> j-1 shift each layer and adds one suffix sum into the rightmost feasible 1-position; implement the shift lazily and the range sum/point update with a Fenwick tree or segment tree.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory.

solution_variants.name: zero-run-subsequence-automaton-dp
solution_variants.summary: Match each target backward by its unique greedy path in a subsequence automaton. A source zero changes its effective matched bit exactly when enough positions to its right have been deleted to reach the next one. The naive (position, deletion-count) state collapses at 1-to-0 transitions to a zero-gap length; process maximal zero-runs, maintain the latest state for each gap length, and use rolling difference sums because adjacent positions in one run differ by only one transition term.

tags.evidence: The main algorithm counts canonical reachable-output prefixes by target length and last greedy endpoint; this recurrence is the central solution.
One output may have many valid interval-endpoint witnesses, but greedily taking the largest feasible p_i gives a unique representative trajectory and prevents duplicate counting.
Each DP layer needs a suffix sum of prior endpoint states followed by one point insertion; range-sum aggregation removes the quadratic transition.
The Luogu implementation maintains point additions and prefix/suffix sums under a global coordinate offset with a Fenwick tree.
The official O(n log n) implementation route maintains the shifted endpoint DP and suffix-sum-to-point transition with a segment tree.
Every layer applies the same index shift to all endpoint states, while only the current rightmost-1 state receives an exceptional recomputed suffix contribution.
Reachability is tested by choosing each decreasing lower endpoint as large as possible, preserving maximal room for all later target characters.
After q operations the l-endpoints are an arbitrary subsequence of 1..n, and the linear alternative counts unique greedy matches in the resulting subsequence automaton.
The O(n) alternative groups equal zero positions into maximal runs; zero-run lengths are exactly the compressed transition states.
The linear solution removes the naive deletion-count dimension by proving that only zero-gap lengths at run boundaries can become relevant.
The alternative counts paths of the canonical backward matcher over source positions and effective output bits.
After zero-run compression, batches of equivalent DP transitions are range additions accumulated by a difference array.
